entry.title: Start Small. Think at Scale. Business Professionals Can Use SAP & Claude to Build a Business Case.
entry.content:
Note: This blog provides an actionable step-by-step proof of concept (POC) for the SAP and Anthropic partnership that a business professional can build today using SAP's free Business Accelerator Hub sandbox data and Claude. The Master Prompt is included below. A sample output is provided in the Addendum. Already familiar with this context? Skip directly to The Proof of Concept section.

Build the POC. Create the Business Case. Start the Conversation.

The recent SAP and Anthropic partnership announcement ignited a new wave of opportunities. It got me thinking about how the combined capabilities of Claude and SAP will strengthen enterprises around the globe.


At the same time, I found myself wondering. How can Business Professionals add real value to what needs to happen to put these capabilities in place for maximum success?

It reminded me of a simple metaphor. Imagine digging a new swimming pool with a shovel. You are making progress. Slowly. Painfully. Then someone pulls up with a bulldozer. The hole that would have taken weeks gets done in a day. That is the uplift the SAP and Anthropic partnership represents for organizations who are ready to act.

So how can Business Professionals help jumpstart the process?

Consider the impact of creating a simple proof of concept using SAP's own sandbox data and Claude, grounded in business case best practices, to help prioritize initiatives that can create compelling value.

The Market Opportunity

The scale of this partnership is significant. SAP and Anthropic are not simply connecting two products. They are building an Autonomous Enterprise vision where Claude powered agents operate through SAP Business AI Platform, coordinate across SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Ariba, and other SAP products via Model Context Protocol (MCP), and are accessible to users through Joule.

IDC projects AI spending driven by agentic AI will reach $1.3 trillion by 2029, growing at 31.9% year over year. In a best case scenario Gartner projects a $450 billion enterprise AI opportunity by 2035. Yet according to the DSAG Investment Report published in February 2026, among SAP customers already using AI, only 3% are running SAP Business AI in production today.

The opportunity is enormous. But before Business Professionals can seize it, they must consider creating POCs and business cases that will move their organization from insight to action.

The New API Policy. A Quality Framework.

It is worth highlighting a recent SAP announcement that is directly relevant to this partnership. SAP's API policy governs how autonomous external agents make sequential API calls into SAP systems.

Consider this approach as a quality framework.

Think Lean Six Sigma, ITIL, or ISO 9001 for mission critical Cloud ERP processes. Controlled variation. Trusted inputs. Governed outputs.

Claude operating inside SAP's approved architecture brings these same quality principles to agentic AI. As a Six Sigma certified practitioner I can say with confidence that controlled variation and governed outputs are not optional in mission critical processes. They are the standard.

Errors in enterprise data cannot be fully eliminated but they must be managed, minimized, and controlled continuously. They stem from uncontrolled inputs, ungoverned processes, and systems operating outside defined boundaries.

SAP's framework controls that variation at the source, reducing risk to the lowest achievable level and keeping mission critical processes operating with the precision enterprises depend on.

From Insight to Action. Building Your Case

Consider this scenario. You are a Business Professional who has just learned about the SAP and Anthropic partnership. You see the opportunity clearly. You know your organization has processes that AI can benefit, especially with Claude and SAP working together.

But where do you start?

You are not a developer. You do not have a project budget approved. You cannot stand up a Joule environment this afternoon.

What you do have is business knowledge, SAP familiarity, and a growing confidence in what Claude can do. Most importantly you have a clear sense of where the pain and inefficiencies are in your organization today.

That is enough to get started.

The path forward has two parallel tracks that reinforce each other:

  1. Delivering a proof of concept (POC) that shows what is possible
  2. Creating a business case that justifies the investment to make it real at scale.

The good news is that SAP has already given you the tools to do both.

The SAP Business Accelerator Hub was built explicitly to accelerate customer time to value. Free to access. No SAP license required. No IT ticket needed. Just a free SAP ID and a clear business question you want Claude to help answer.

The proof of concept you build today becomes the evidence that funds the enterprise deployment tomorrow. The business case you build around it gives your organization's executive sponsors the confidence to act.

This approach is how Business Professionals provide meaningful leadership, collaborating with IT counterparts and key stakeholders to move from insight to action.

The Proof of Concept  

This POC is intentionally simple and uses SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition procurement data available through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox. It is a minimum viable product (MVP) approach designed to show what is possible without requiring engineering support, IT involvement, or a live SAP system. Every step is something a Business Professional can execute independently.

What you are about to build is a working example of agentic AI. Claude is not simply answering a question. It is reasoning over real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition data, identifying risk, prioritizing action, and recommending next steps. That is agentic AI in action. And it is a preview of what runs inside Joule at enterprise scale.

The following steps walk you through the POC from start to finish.

What You Do

  1. Register for a free SAP ID at api.sap.com. Takes a few minutes.
  2. Go to the Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001) in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This is the current OData V4 Purchase Order API for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and one example of the many sandbox APIs available across SAP's core product portfolio.
  3. Click “Try Out” at the top. Select “Sandbox” from the dropdown on the right side. Navigate to the Purchase Order section in the left hand navigation. Click the /PurchaseOrder “GET” option. Click “Run.”
  4. Copy the JSON response that appears under “Response Body.”
  5. Open Claude at claude.ai. Paste the data and enter the Master Prompt below. Note: A free Claude account is sufficient for this POC. Claude Pro provides higher usage limits if needed.

What Claude Does

  1. Reads and interprets the SAP S/4HANA Cloud purchase order data.
  2. Identifies which exceptions represent the highest business risk.
  3. Assigns a risk score to each record with a clear rationale.
  4. Recommends specific actions for each prioritized item.
  5. Produces a scored table ready for your business case conversation.
  6. Offers to draft a supplier communication for the highest risk item.

Master Prompt

Copy and paste the following prompt into Claude along with your JSON data (as noted above). You are reviewing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition purchase order data retrieved directly from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox via the Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001).

[START PROMPT]

Review the records provided and analyze the following fields: PurchaseOrder number, Supplier, CreationDate, PurchaseOrderDate, PurchasingProcessingStatus, PurchaseOrderDeletionCode, ReleaseIsNotCompleted, and PurchasingReleaseStrategy.

Based on your analysis:

  1. Identify purchase orders that require attention. Focus on deletion flagged records, incomplete releases, unusual processing statuses, and supplier concentration patterns.
  2. Assign a priority level of High, Medium, or Low to each flagged record with a brief rationale.
  3. Recommend a specific action for each flagged item.
  4. Produce a prioritized table with the following columns: PO Number, Supplier, Creation Date, Processing Status, Deletion Flag, Release Complete, Priority Level, Recommended Action.
  5. Provide a two to three sentence plain English summary of your overall findings before the table.
  6. After the table offer to draft a supplier communication for the highest priority item.
  7. Keep your total response concise. Summary, table, and offer only. No additional commentary unless asked.

[END PROMPT]

What You Just Built

A working example of agentic AI reasoning over real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition procurement data. Built by a Business Professional. Using SAP's own tools. View the sample output in the Addendum below

This is your building block. The cornerstone of your business case. And the start of a meaningful conversation with your IT counterparts, key stakeholders, and executive sponsors about what is possible and the value this use case can deliver.

Start small. Think at scale.

From POC to Production

This POC uses the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox as a starting point outside of SAP's production architecture. That is intentional. The goal is to demonstrate the concept quickly and accessibly. Free sandbox data. No infrastructure required.

Joule Studio is where developers and business teams can go next. A governed, enterprise grade environment where Business Professionals and developers collaborate to build both pre-built and custom agents tailored to their specific processes, data, and workflows. Joule Studio requires SAP BTP, a Joule subscription, SAP Build licensing, and IT provisioning to activate. It is the right next step. See Activate Joule Studio in 5 Simple Steps for full prerequisites.  

Complementary approaches. Each with its own role.

In production this same capability runs natively inside SAP Business AI Platform through the MCP Gateway. It is accessible to users through Joule. Everything you just demonstrated is the reasoning capability that sits behind the potential production implementation. The logic transfers directly.

In this POC the Hub provides everything you need to show what is possible.

Building the Business Case

A strong business case requires the right elements addressed with clarity and evidence. The POC you just built directly addresses two of the most critical. It defines the problem in concrete terms and demonstrates measurable value, ROI potential, and a clear view of what is possible.

The implementation path and risk and governance elements are addressed by SAP's approved architecture. The path runs through SAP Business AI Platform, Joule, and the production framework SAP and Anthropic have built together.

That represents a credible and governed foundation for any business case conversation.


Security and Data You Can Depend On

The same security and governance framework that protects your SAP environment today protects every action Claude takes inside it. The controls do not change because AI is involved. They extend to cover it.

Every action is governed by the same role-based access controls your organization operates under today. Every decision Claude makes is traceable through a full audit trail.

Connecting external data sources to the SAP environment requires proper integration through the SAP Integration Suite, ensuring data flows through governed, secure pathways before Claude ever touches it.

The Path Forward

The SAP and Anthropic partnership is designed for enterprise scale, grounded in trust and quality outcomes. Today, Business Professionals can create a tangible starting point and play a leadership role in the conversation with IT and business stakeholders about initiatives that can create lasting value.

SAP and Anthropic have announced plans to bring Claude to SAP Business AI Platform. Joule Studio, the development environment where agents are built and deployed, has general availability planned for later this year.

Now is the time to build your POC and business case so your organization is ready.

Take it one step at a time.

Start small. Think at scale.

Join the Conversation

Your likes and comments are welcome and appreciated.

  • Are you excited about the SAP and Anthropic partnership?
  • Were you aware of the SAP Business Accelerator Hub?
  • What are you working on with Claude or your favorite Assistant AI that can be applied to the Autonomous Enterprise vision?

About the Author

Rich Blumberg is an award-winning writer and author of two Amazon published books, Novi’s Journey to the Magic Garden and Job Seeking Warriors – A Mentors Guide to Winning. He's the President of World Sales Solutions, LLC, (WSS) a management consulting, low-code, no-code, and AI services firm. For more than 20 years he has worked as an SAP consultant partnering with SAP and their ecosystem organizations around the globe. Outside of work Rich serves as a Drexel University Alumni Board of Governors Emeritus, an ENGin English tutor supporting Ukrainian citizens, and an aspiring guitar player.

Related Blogs

  • How the AI Content Lifecycle Turns Complexity into Clarity at Scale(Part 2)
  • AI-Powered Knowledge Gap Analysis and Content Lifecycle Governance(Part 1)
  • You Can Build an Agentic AI Workflow. No Coding, No Engineering, No Problem.
  • AI at Warp Speed: Lessons from the Horse and Buggy Collapse

Addendum SAP S/4HANA Cloud POC Output

Purchase Order Analysis using the SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox and Claude

Summary

Of the 50 purchase orders reviewed, 6 require immediate attention. Two records carry both a deletion flag and an incomplete release status, representing the highest risk. Four additional records show a deletion code with a completed processing status, suggesting they were cancelled but may warrant confirmation. Supplier 17300001 dominates the dataset with over 60% concentration, which itself warrants a supplier risk review.

Note: In a production environment this same analysis can run across thousands of purchase orders in seconds.

Prioritized Purchase Order Exception Table

Draft Supplier Communication

Highest Priority Item: Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022 | Supplier USSU-VSF01

Subject: Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022 – Urgent Status Clarification Required

Dear USSU-VSF01 Team,

I am writing regarding two purchase orders currently showing an unresolved status in our SAP S/4HANA system that require your immediate attention.

Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022, both dated August 18, 2016, are currently flagged for deletion while the approval release process remains incomplete. This combination of statuses prevents us from properly closing or reactivating these orders and creates a compliance risk in our procurement records.

We kindly request that you confirm the following by end of business this week:

  1. Whether any goods or services were delivered against either purchase order.
  2. Whether any outstanding invoices or payment obligations remain.
  3. Your agreement to formally close both orders if no outstanding obligations exist.

Please contact our procurement team at your earliest convenience so we can resolve this matter and ensure our records accurately reflect the current state of our relationship.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this request.

Best regards,
Procurement Team
Company Code 1710

Data source: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001) via SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox. Analysis performed by

entry.title: From Fragmented Landscapes to Unified Insights: Business Consent in SAP BDC
entry.content:
Organizations often discover that their SAP landscapes are spread across multiple CRM IDs just when they want to activate cross-solution scenarios like People Intelligence on SAP Business Data Cloud. This article walks through how Business Consent helps you overcome those CRM boundaries and quickly connect SAP BDC with say SAP SuccessFactors (any of our Public Cloud or RISE portfolio), without waiting for lengthy consolidation projects.

I would like to share some of the key learnings from SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) deep-dive design discussions and implementation experiences that may benefit customers and partners in the future.

Let us assume you are a customer using SAP SuccessFactors and are interested in exploring People Intelligence. You sign the order form for SAP BDC, and this order form grants you the necessary cloud services such as SAP BDC Core and includes a line item for People Intelligence, listing the number of users and the corresponding BDC Capacity Units to which you are entitled.

All these entitlements are grouped under a CRM ID, for example 100001. Since you already have SAP SuccessFactors, let us say it is under CRM ID 100002.

Once your SAP BDC tenant is provisioned, you proceed to add the relevant BDC components and the SuccessFactors system as part of the system formation (refer to these SAP BDC Provisioning blog series  by Shreya, where she provides an excellent explanation of the steps; Step 21 covers system formation). This is one of the mandatory steps required to deploy and use People Intelligence applications and their corresponding data products. When you deploy an app, system formation acts as the enabler for harmonizing data from the source system using SAP-managed pipelines, which in turn deploy data products and intelligent apps into BDC. For system formation, one of the expectations is that all landscapes, including the provisioned BDC tenant, fall under the same CRM ID. If this is not the case, you can still provision BDC and perform system formation for the BDC components, but since SuccessFactors is on 1000002, you will not be able to add it to the system formation.

When we review the order form and validate the CRM IDs, it is often the case that systems are distributed across multiple CRM IDs. While it is technically possible to consolidate all systems into a single CRM ID, this process may take several weeks or even months. This is precisely where the concept of Business Consent becomes valuable and helps you avoid that additional effort. Let me explain this with a scenario.

Based on the earlier example, from SAP4ME I have already added the BDC components as part of system formation, and these reside under CRM ID 100001.

Next, I attempt to include new systems in the system formation and search for the SuccessFactors system, but I am unable to find it because it is under a different CRM ID.

To have this system included in the system formation, I navigate to the "Consent Requests" section under the Customer Landscape tab in SAP4ME and then select "Request Consent".

You can either provide only the SuccessFactors system URL or specify the System ID and namespace, and then click "Request".

If you need to determine the System ID and namespace, here is an example of how I obtain them from the Systems Landscape under BTP for SuccessFactors. Namespaces will always follow the pattern sap.<cloudsystem>; in this case, it is sf. You then choose the scope as "System Sharing".

Once you click "Request", the consent request is sent to the BTP admin of the SuccessFactors landscape.

They can review the incoming consent request from their BTP Cockpit under the Consent Request section of the system landscape. Under the "Incoming Consent Requests" tab, the admin can either approve or reject the consent request.

After the request is approved, the BDC admin can view the status in SAP4ME under the customer landscape, where it will appear with the status "Approved".

At this point, the consent between BDC and the source system in a different CRM ID has been successfully established. When you now attempt to add the SuccessFactors system as part of the system formation, you will be able to find it, as the approved system from the consent request appears in the "Include Systems" list.

This approach eliminates the need to consolidate systems under a single CRM ID and still allows you to access systems that reside in different CRM IDs.

Business Consent offers a pragmatic path to realizing value from SAP Business Data Cloud even when your systems sit in different CRM IDs. By leveraging consent requests and system sharing, you can accelerate People Intelligence use cases, reduce landscape friction, and focus your energy on delivering insights rather than restructuring your commercial setup.

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud and BAS
entry.content:
Machine Learning on structured data might not be what many people have in mind when talking about AI. Yet, it is a form of AI and the massive interest in Large Language Models, agents and assistants is also leading to many new Machine Learning Projects. 

With this blog you can get hands-on with Machine Learning by implementing a time-series forecast in SAP HANA Cloud. In the requirements and projects that I am seeing, time-series forecasting is by far the most popular Machine Learning approach in a SAP related project. From Accounts Receivable forecasting, to demand forecasting and even solar power prediction, a time-series forecast might get you there.

IMPORTANT: You can download all code from this repository.

 

Architecture

The architecture for this example is as straightforward as it can be. Your data is in SAP HANA Cloud, where we also have Machine Learning algorithms (Predictive Analysis Library and Automated Predictive Library). We then use Python in a Jupyter Notebook running in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger the Machine Learning algorithms in SAP HANA Cloud. This is possible with SAP's hana_ml Python package. You can also choose any other Python environment of your choice to use that package to trigger the built-in algorithms. No data ever has to leave SAP HANA Cloud, since data and ML algorithm are already in the same place.

This blog focuses mostly on a stand-alone SAP HANA Cloud. However, you can also trigger the same Machine Learning in SAP Datasphere's embedded SAP HANA Cloud. This blog has more details. 

More components are typically needed for a productive environment. To automate the creation of predictions the Python code can be schedules for instance with Cloud Foundry or SAP AI Core. And the time-series algorithm we are using is just one of 100+ algorithms for you to choose from. Here is an overview. See the hana_ml documentation for details.

 

Prerequisites

This blog assumes that:

  • You have SAP Business Application Studio up and running and configured as described in "Setting up Python in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger HANA Cloud Machine Learning"
  • You have access to a SAP HANA Cloud instance, that is configured as described in the same above blog.

 

Data Load

You will use historic data from the Swiss tourism area to predict how many nights people will spend in a hotel each month in the next 12 months. Download the file HOTELNIGHTS.csv and import it into SAP HANA Cloud, for instance with the SAP HANA Database Explorer. Select "Import Data".

 

Click through the options:

  • name the new table: HOTELNIGHTS
  • set the following column data types
    • MONTH: Date
    • ARRIVALS: Integer
    • HOTELNIGHTS: Integer

 

You will see that the information in that table is quite detailed. It shows for the period from January 2020 to March 2026 by month how many people from which Country spent how many nights in a hotel in the various regions of Switzerland.

 

Time-series forecast

Now use the data for a time-series forecast! Script in Python and the hana_ml package translates your logic into SQL and has SAP HANA Cloud do the work. 

IMPORTANT: You can download the notebook HANAML time series forecast.ipynb, which has the following code included. If you upload that file into your environment and execute the first cell, you will be prompted to select the Python environment. This can be easily overlooked...

If you are meeting the prerequisites (see above), you should already be able to connect form a Jupyter notebook in BAS to connect to your SAP HANA Cloud. Continue now by storing the database user's details a in a credentials file.

{
  "SAP_HANA_CLOUD": {
    "HANA_ADDRESS": "YOURENDPOINT",
    "HANA_PORT": 443,
    "HANA_USER": "AIUSER",
    "HANA_PASSWORD": "YOURSECRETPASSWORD"
  }
}

 

Load these credentials in Python and verify that the connection still returns True.

import json
with open('./credentials.json', 'r') as creds:
  credentials = json.load(creds)
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS  = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_ADDRESS"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PORT"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_USER"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PASSWORD"]
import hana_ml.dataframe as dataframe
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(address  = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS,
                                   port     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT,
                                   user     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER,
                                   password = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD)
conn.connection.isconnected()

In case you see a compatibility warning for Python 3.0.2, feel free to ignore it.

Continue by creating a hana_ml DataFrame, which points to the Data in SAP HANA Cloud. The data remains in SAP HANA Cloud. No records have yet left the system. This is by default and on purpose. The intention is to keep the data in the system and do any data processing within SAP HANA Cloud.

data_hdf = conn.table('HOTELNIGHTS')

 

It is possible to retrieve data though. You just need to request it with the collect()-method, which downloads the data of the hana_ml DataFrame as Pandas DataFrame. To avoid downloading all records, first restrict the data to 5 rows with the head()-method. The sequence is very important here. First reduce the number of records, then download.

data_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

To get a more detailed feel for the data, have some statistics calculated.

data_hdf.describe().collect()

 

There are 13 Swiss regions in the data for example and the maximum number of hotel nights in a specific months in a specific region from a specific country is 223091. Important here is the fact that every value in that table was calculated in SAP HANA Cloud. Imagine you are working with a massive table, you will want to leverage the processing power of SAP HANA Cloud. That's ensured here, since the hana_ml package created the necessary SQL statement. That SQL syntax can be seen by adding ".select_statement" to the command. The screenshot shows only a fraction of the SQL.

import pprint
pprint.pp(data_hdf.describe().select_statement)

 

One of many other ways to explore the data is an interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(data_hdf).build().display()

 

We are happy with the data and need to prepare it now for a monthly forecast of all hotel nights in the country. Aggregate by month. 

agg_hdf = data_hdf.agg([('sum', 'HOTELNIGHTS', 'HOTELNIGHTS')], group_by='MONTH')
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.sort('MONTH')
agg_hdf.head(5).collect()

As usual, that aggregation is also done on SAP HANA Cloud and only 5 aggregated values are downloaded. Also as usual, you could add ".select_statement" to see the exact SQL syntax that is used.

Plot the monthly data to get a visual impression for the data. This requires the installation of another Python package. Run this cell once. After the package is installed:

  • you may want to delete the cell (or comment it out) to avoid unnecessary reinstallation attempts)
  • and you may need to restart the Python kernel so that the new package can be used. Click the "Restart" icon in the menu on top and run the earlier cells again.
!pip install nbformat # Once installed, turn this line into a comment to avoid running it again

 

Download the monthly aggregates and create a line chart.

import plotly.express as px
data_df = agg_hdf.collect()
fig =  px.line(data_df, x='MONTH', y='HOTELNIGHTS', title='Hotel nights by Month')
fig.show()

There is a beautiful repeating pattern in the data. True seasonality. Peaks in the summer, lows in November and lower peaks in winter. Train a time-series model! We use the AdditiveModelForecast which is an implementation of Meta's Prophet algorithm.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.tsa.additive_model_forecast import AdditiveModelForecast
amf = AdditiveModelForecast()
amf.fit(data=agg_hdf)

 

Explore the trained model in another interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(amf).build().display()

 

With that trained model we can forecast the unknown future. Just prepare a hana_ml DataFrame that contains the months (or dates), for which we want forecasts. In a productive scenario you will probably connect to a table that's permanently updated with new records. Hence, to keep the code dynamic for scheduling, find out from the data the most recent month for which data is available. 

str_lastdate = agg_hdf.tail(1, ref_col='MONTH').collect().iloc[0,0]
str_lastdate = str(str_lastdate)[0:10]
print(str_lastdate)

 

Use that month as basis create the required dates for the forecast. We need 12 records, always the first day of the following 12 months.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.random import binomial
months_to_forecast=12
future_hdf = binomial(conn, n=1, p=1, num_random=months_to_forecast)
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('*', (f'''ADD_MONTHS(TO_DATE ('{str_lastdate}', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), ID+1)''', 'MONTH') )
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('MONTH', ('0', 'TARGET'))
future_hdf.head(20).collect()

 

Apply the trained model to predict those dates. And you get the prediction (YHAT) as well as the prediction interval (YHAT_LOWER and YHAT_UPPER).

predicted_hdf = amf.predict(data=future_hdf)
predicted_hdf.head(20).collect()

Before using the predictions, have a closer look at the trained model and the forecasted values in the standard report.

 

The outlier tab points out a few months in 2020 that are unusual. Indeed, there was something that year that impacted travelling and hotel nights....  Cutting off that old history would be a good idea. You could go back to the top of the Notebook and add a filter to the data, ie 

data_hdf = data_hdf.filter("YEAR(MONTH) >= 2022")

 

In this tutorial we continue with the model as it is. You could save the predictions just as they are. However, you may also want to combine the past actuals with the predictions into a single table since that might make it easier to put a dashboard on top for instance.

predicted_hdf = predicted_hdf.select('MONTH',
                                    ('NULL', 'HOTELNIGHTS'),
                                    ('YHAT', 'FORECAST'),
                                    ('YHAT_LOWER', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                                    ('YHAT_UPPER', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.select('*', 
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
actualsandpred_hdf = predicted_hdf.union(agg_hdf)
actualsandpred_hdf.sort('MONTH').tail(5).collect()

 

Finally, save that combined data into a table in SAP HANA Cloud. In case that table doesn't exist yet, it will be automatically created in the format of the hana_ml DataFrame.

actualsandpred_hdf.save('HOTELNIGHTS_FORECAST', force=True)

 

With this you have enriched your data in SAP HANA Cloud through Machine Learning! The actuals in the HOTELNIGHTS column end in March 2026 with the FORECAST continuing on from April 2026 to March 2027.

 

Related topics

With such a forecast you can already achieve a lot. You may also want to extend it further or use a completely different algorithm. These resources can help you along, but feel free to ask if you have any questions!

  • Documentation hana_ml package
  • Documentation of the underlying Predictive Analysis Library
  • Sample code for the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) and also the Automated Predictive Library (APL)

Or if you want to have the code written for you, check out this "Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline".

entry.title: From Agent to MCP: What Building an MCP Taught Me About the Future of AI Integration
entry.content:
1. The moment that sparked the question

I've been building automations for a few years now. Not because someone asked me to — because I genuinely wanted to see if it worked.

It started with RPA. And honestly, for what it was, it worked. It solved real problems. But every time a process changed, the bot broke. Every time something shifted upstream, I was back in the designer fixing selectors and re-mapping fields. It was useful — just fragile in a way that bothered me.

Then I added LLMs to the mix. Suddenly the automation could handle variability, could read a document instead of just clicking through a form. That was exciting. But now I had two systems with completely different failure modes sitting next to each other, and I was the glue.

So I went further. I built a custom agent from scratch — a proper one, with skills, context management, decision logic. I wrote about it here. That project taught me more about how AI systems actually behave than anything I'd read. And when it worked, it really worked.

But there's always a next thing to build.

When Joule Work Desktop was announced at Sapphire, MCP was the integration standard. I didn't overthink it — if I wanted to connect to Joule, that was the path. What I didn't expect was how much building an MCP would make me question everything I thought I already understood.

The surface looks familiar: you're still giving a model tools to call, still thinking about context, still asking yourself what the model needs to know to be useful. But the architecture underneath is different in ways that matter. And those differences aren't obvious until you're in the middle of building.

This post is about what I found there.

2. Two ways to give an LLM context: Agents with Skills vs. MCP

Both approaches solve the same fundamental problem: the model doesn't know anything about your system unless you tell it.

You define what tools exist. You describe what they do. The model decides when and how to use them. That mechanic is identical in both. If you've built one, the mental model for the other isn't foreign. You're not starting from zero.

That's where the similarity ends — and where it gets interesting.

What surprised me about the agent

I'll be honest: I went into the agent build fully expecting to suffer.

Years of writing integrations the hard way had trained me well. I had my mental checklist ready: parse the API response, map the fields, pass the values explicitly between steps, handle the edge cases nobody documented. I was prepared for a week of plumbing before I could write a single line of actual logic.

Then I typed "for each result, do this" — and the agent just... did it. A proper for-each. No code. No explicit loop. I called an API and the response landed in context already mapped, already interpretable, ready to use in the next step. The model looked at the shape of the data and figured it out.

I sat there for a second genuinely unsure if it had worked or if I was about to discover a very elegant failure.

It had worked.

That moment reset a lot of assumptions I didn't know I was carrying. The plumbing I had prepared to build never needed to exist. What was left was only the part that actually required my brain: the business rules, the scoring logic, the decision criteria. The stuff that actually matters.

What's different about MCP

With the custom agent, I was still the director. The agent had skills, the skills called APIs, I defined the flow, I could see everything happening. It was my system behaving the way I told it to.

MCP is a different mental model — and it took me a moment to fully land on it.

You build a server. You expose tools. And then you let go.

The client — in this case Joule Desktop — connects to your server, discovers what you've built, and decides how to use it based on what the user asks. You don't orchestrate the flow. You don't manage the context. You define the capability and the client figures out the rest.

The first time I really internalized that, my instinct was mild panic. What do you mean I don't control the flow? But then something clicked: I wasn't building an agent anymore. I was building an interface. A set of capabilities that any MCP-compatible client could plug into — today Joule, tomorrow something else entirely. That's not losing control. That's actually a better kind of architecture.

The real difference: who's in charge

  Custom Agent (Skills) MCP

Who orchestratesThe agentThe client (Joule, Claude, etc.)
Where context livesInside the agentInside the client
Who can use the toolsOnly that agentAny MCP-compatible client
DeploymentPlatform-boundIndependent server
What you controlEverythingThe tools and their outputs

Neither is better. The agent gave me more control and visibility — which was exactly right for a workflow that needed precise, predictable steps. MCP gave me portability and decoupling — which was exactly right for something I wanted any compatible client to use without rebuilding from scratch.

The question isn't which one is superior. The question is what you're actually trying to build.

 

3. What surprised me while building

The design changed three times. And that was the real lesson.

When I start a new server, my brain goes straight to the same place it always does: define the endpoints, figure out the data model, write the code. I've done it enough times that it's almost automatic.

That instinct completely failed me here.

The first version had too many tools, each doing too little. Classic API thinking — small, focused, composable. The kind of thing that looks great in a diagram and makes you feel like a responsible adult. The model looked at my beautiful diagram and chose the wrong tool anyway. Every time. It was combining things I hadn't anticipated, skipping steps that seemed obvious, and generally behaving like it had never seen my very clean diagram. Because it hadn't. Because it doesn't work that way.

I went back in. Second version: fewer tools, each doing more. The model seemed happier. I was not. I now had tools that behaved differently depending on the input, and I couldn't clearly explain what they did even to myself. That's usually a sign something has gone wrong.

Third version — something finally clicked. And what changed wasn't the code. It was how I was thinking about the whole thing.

You're not designing endpoints for a developer to call. You're designing capabilities for a model to reason about. The name you give a tool shapes when the model reaches for it. The description isn't documentation — it's an instruction. You're writing for a different kind of reader, and once that landed for me, the design stopped fighting back.

A rule I wish someone had told me on day one: don't start building a tool until you can describe it in one sentence to a non-developer. If you can't explain it clearly, the model won't figure it out either. Write the descriptions before you write the code. Yes, before.

Then I ran into the actual hard part. And it wasn't the server.

The server was running. The tools were working. I was feeling pretty good about myself.

And then someone asked a real question and the answer came back technically correct, well-structured, and completely useless.

That's the failure mode nobody puts in the tutorial. A model without the right context doesn't throw an error — it generates a confident, beautifully formatted response that says absolutely nothing of value. It sounds like it knows what it's talking about. It doesn't. And that's scarier than a stack trace because at least a stack trace tells you something is broken.

I ended up spending more time going back to fix the knowledge layer than I'd spent building the server in the first place. Cleaning structure, rethinking what actually needed to be there versus what was just noise dressed up as content. All work that would have been faster — and less humbling — if I'd treated it as the first problem to solve instead of the thing I'd get to eventually.

The lesson, painfully earned: LLMs don't create knowledge. They surface it. Put noise in, get noise back — in a very professional tone. The knowledge layer is the foundation. The server is just the delivery mechanism. I had those two things backwards for longer than I'd like to admit.

The part I didn't expect to enjoy: vector search

I'll be honest — before this project, "embeddings" was one of those words I nodded at confidently without fully understanding.

The vector search itself went smoother than expected. Once it was running it felt almost like cheating — you send a plain question in natural language and the right content comes back ranked by actual meaning, not keyword overlap. No brittle query strings. No AND/OR logic. Just... the right stuff, most of the time.

But getting there sent me down a rabbit hole I had not budgeted for. What embeddings actually are. Why the model you use to generate them changes what comes back. Why chunking strategy — how you split and index the source content — matters way more than it sounds like it should. Why two distance metrics that seem equivalent can return completely different results.

I didn't plan for any of that. I came out of it genuinely glad I went in — and slightly annoyed that nobody had handed me a map.

4. MCP on SAP BTP: the trust boundary as a feature

There's a version of this that lives somewhere else. A different cloud provider, external APIs for embeddings, a third-party vector database. It would have worked technically.

I didn't build that version.

The decision to run the MCP on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry wasn't just about where to host something. It was about where the knowledge base lives — and what that means when you're building inside an enterprise ecosystem.

The institutional knowledge powering the server comes from SAP sources. Running on BTP meant that authentication, connectivity, and data access were all handled through mechanisms that already existed and were already trusted. No external service credentials to manage. No custom connectors to build. No explaining to anyone why sensitive knowledge was touching a third-party API.

That last one matters more than it sounds.

In enterprise settings, the conversation about data governance can easily become longer than the conversation about the actual product. Running inside the platform's trust boundary meant that conversation was mostly resolved before it started. The data doesn't leave the ecosystem. Embeddings are generated through services already inside it. The vector search runs natively. The MCP sits on Cloud Foundry. It's the same platform all the way through — and in this context, that's a feature, not a constraint.

Using the same platform as both a data store and a vector engine was the piece that made the architecture feel coherent. I wasn't stitching together the best individual tool for each layer and hoping they'd talk to each other cleanly. One platform, one trust model, one less thing to explain to anyone who asked.

The boundary wasn't something I worked around. It was something I designed with.

5. Under the hood

I want to keep this section honest and specific. Not a sales pitch for any particular stack — just the actual pieces, how they connect, and the decisions that shaped them. 

Design

The server itself

The MCP is built in Python using FastMCP — the high-level wrapper from the official MCP SDK. If you've never used it, the experience is surprisingly close to writing a FastAPI app: you define functions, decorate them with @McP.tool(), write a proper docstring, and that's your tool. The docstring isn't optional ceremony — it's what the model reads to decide when and how to call the function. Treat it like an API contract written for a reader who can't ask clarifying questions.

@mcp.tool()
def retrieve_similar_cases(query: str, category: str, top_k: int = 3) -> str:
    """
    Retrieve similar historical cases from the vector store.
    Returns expert-validated signals: category, effort, timeline, impact.
    Use these as directional hints — NOT as the final answer.
    """
    ...

The vector search

This is where the retrieval happens. The core is a HANA Cloud query using COSINE_SIMILARITY against a REAL_VECTOR column — filtered by a dimension, ordered by semantic proximity:

SELECT TOP 3 *
FROM KNOWLEDGE_TABLE
WHERE CATEGORY = ?
ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC

Before this query runs, the incoming text gets converted into an embedding vector via SAP AI Core. That vector is what gets compared against the stored embeddings in HANA. The model that generates the embeddings matters — consistency between ingestion time and query time is non-negotiable. If you embed your knowledge base with model A and query with model B, the cosine similarities will be meaningless. Same model, both directions, always.

For batch processing, the searches run in parallel using ThreadPoolExecutor — one connection per thread, all results collected before returning. The difference in latency between sequential and parallel retrieval across a large batch is significant enough that this wasn't optional.

Two vector indexes, one query pattern

The knowledge base has two separate tables, each with its own EMBEDDING column and its own filter logic. Both are queried using the same cosine similarity pattern, just with different filters and column mappings. The retrieval function runs both searches concurrently and returns a unified result keyed by source type:

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(source_types)) as pool:
    results = dict(pool.map(_search_one, source_types))

Simple, but effective. The model gets both result sets in a single tool call and uses them independently when synthesizing the response.

The transport layer on BTP

On Cloud Foundry, the server runs as a standard Python app bound to a port via the $PORT environment variable. No custom buildpack, no containers — just a Procfile, a manifest.yml, and the app itself. The MCP endpoint is exposed over HTTPS at the app's route, which is what Joule Desktop connects to. Authentication and routing are handled by BTP's built-in infrastructure.

The only thing worth calling out: stateless_http=True in the FastMCP init. MCP sessions over HTTP are stateless by design here — each request carries its own context. That's the right model for a server that multiple clients might connect to simultaneously, and it's what made the BTP deployment straightforward without needing session affinity or sticky routing.

6. Where MCP fits in the SAP AI ecosystem

I want to be careful here not to oversell something I'm still learning. But I also think there's something real happening and it's worth saying out loud.

MCP is gaining momentum inside the SAP ecosystem faster than most integration standards do. And I think the reason is simpler than it seems: it solves a problem that everyone building with AI on SAP has been quietly struggling with — how do you give a model structured access to enterprise capabilities without building a custom integration every single time?

Skills were a good answer. They worked. But they were tightly coupled to a specific agent, on a specific platform, for a specific use case. Every new scenario meant starting over.

MCP decouples that. You build the capability once. Any compatible client can use it. Today that's Joule Desktop. Tomorrow it'll be whatever comes after Joule Desktop, and the MCP you built won't care. That's the kind of architectural bet that tends to age well.

Joule Work Desktop is already in pilot. MCP is the integration path they chose. That's not a coincidence — that's a direction. And the developers who understand how to build MCPs well right now, before it's mainstream, before there are established patterns and ready-made templates for everything, are going to have a significant head start on everyone who waits until it's the obvious thing to do.

I'm not saying drop everything and rewrite your agents as MCPs. I'm saying: if you've been curious, now is a genuinely good time to build something small and see what you learn. The protocol is approachable. The tooling is improving fast. And the mental shift it forces — from thinking about functions to thinking about capabilities a model can reason about — is one of those shifts that changes how you see the whole problem space, not just this one project.

Build something. Even if it's small. Especially if it's small.

The best way to understand what MCP actually changes is to be in the middle of building with it and suddenly realize your old instincts aren't working anymore. That moment of friction is where the learning is. Don't skip it by reading about it — go find it.

entry.title: Get More from SAP HANA Cloud: Join the Intelligent Advisor CEI
entry.content:
If you're running SAP HANA Cloud, there's a good chance your system already has the tools to optimize itself - you just haven't had the chance to explore them yet. That's exactly what this Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) is designed to change.

What Is This CEI?

We're inviting selected SAP HANA Cloud customers to work directly with SAP product experts in a hands-on exploration of the Recommendation App and its Advisors. No additional licensing. No new tools to buy. Just practical guidance to get more value from what you already have.

This is a two-way collaboration: you get expert knowledge tailored to your landscape, and we get the real-world feedback we need to build a better product.

The Advisors in Focus

The Recommendation App includes four advisors, each targeting a different dimension of system performance:

  • Partition Advisor — identifies tables that would benefit from partitioning to improve query performance and manageability
  • Index Advisor — recommends creating and dropping indexes based on your actual workload patterns
  • NSE Advisor — helps optimize NSE (Native Storage Extension) usage to balance performance and memory cost
  • Performance Class Advisor — assists in selecting the optimal performance class or instance size based on historical workload patterns (2026.QRC2 GA planned)

Together, these advisors surface concrete, actionable recommendations — but knowing how to evaluate and apply them in your specific landscape makes all the difference.

What You'll Get Out of It

  • A clear understanding of what each advisor does and the value it can deliver for your system
  • Hands-on guidance from SAP product experts on how to act on recommendations
  • A direct channel to share feedback that shapes the product roadmap

What We're Looking to Learn

We want to understand:

  • What's working well and what isn't
  • Where technical, operational, or conceptual blockers prevent customers from acting on recommendations
  • What improvements would make the advisors more useful in real-world scenarios

Your feedback directly influences our prioritization decisions.

How It Works

The engagement is lightweight by design — ~4 hours per month — structured across three touchpoints:

  1. Initial Call — introduction, landscape review, and goal alignment
  2. Feedback Call — hands-on exploration and open discussion
  3. Final Closing Call — summary, findings, and next steps

Who Should Join?

This CEI is ideal for SAP HANA Cloud customers who:

  • Are interested in optimizing system performance without additional investment
  • Have not yet explored the Recommendation App in depth
  • Want a direct line to SAP product experts and the ability to influence the roadmap

Ready to Participate?

If you're interested in joining or want to learn more, reach out to your SAP account team or sign up directly through the CEI project page.

Apply to Join the CEI

This Customer Engagement Initiative is part of SAP's ongoing commitment to co-innovating with customers. Participation is by invitation and at no additional cost.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere: Data Tiering Strategy and Best Practices
entry.content:
Introduction:

Data tiering is becoming a critical pillar of every organization’s data and analytics strategy. As companies move toward AI-driven decision-making and deploy AI agents, MCP servers, and other autonomous capabilities, the value of context-rich, trusted data products increases dramatically. Simply put: high‑quality data is the fuel that powers generative, agentic, and autonomous applications across the business.A modern data tiering (or data management) strategy is about organizing and storing data in a way that optimizes both cost and performance. Data is being generated at unprecedented speed and volume, from business transactions and master data to IoT signals, application logs, surveys, social media, and streaming sources. Not all of this data is equally valuable at all times, and not all of it needs the same level of performance or freshness. The key is to balance the cost of storing data with the cost (and business value) of accessing it.

Different consumption patterns drive different requirements:

  • Real-time operational analytics (daily, weekly)
  • Tactical reporting (monthly, quarterly)
  • Strategic analysis (multi-year historical trends)
  • Advanced AI and machine learning use cases

For each of these, it is essential to understand what data tiering is, which options exist, and in which scenarios to apply which tier. In this technical article, we focus on data tiering in the context of SAP’s Business Data Cloud, with a particular emphasis on SAP Datasphere. We align the discussion with SAP’s latest data and analytics strategy as presented at Sapphire 2026, and provide practical guidance and best practices for designing an effective tiering strategy that supports both traditional analytics and next-generation AI workloads.

1. Business & AI Context:

AI is no longer an experimental add‑on to the SAP landscape; it is becoming the execution layer of the enterprise. With SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents, assistants, and Joule-powered workflows are expected to automate large parts of finance, supply chain, HR, and customer operations. All of that depends on one thing: trusted, well‑managed data with rich business context.

1.1 Why Data Tiering Matters in an AI-First World?

Modern AI in SAP is context-hungry. It does not just need a lot of data; it needs: Who the customer  is across CRM, S/4HANA, and e‑commerce, Which material or location a transaction relates to, What policies or controls apply in a given process or region, How a process (order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, hire-to-retire) is supposed to run. This business context is what SAP brings to AI, and it is exactly what SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere are designed to provide. However, the data feeding AI agents, planning models, and analytics is exploding in volume and variety:

  • Core ERP transactions and master data
  • IoT, machine telemetry, and sensor streams
  •  Application logs and clickstreams
  • Survey, social, and interaction data
  • External market and partner data

Not all of this data needs to sit on the most expensive, highest‑performance storage. At the same time, some scenarios, like Joule agents making operational decisions, cannot tolerate high latency or missing data. Data tiering is how you manage this trade‑off between cost and performance without compromising AI quality and trust.

1.2 From Reporting to Autonomous Decisions

Historically, data tiering conversations in SAP environments focused on:

  • Reducing database size (especially in ERP and BW)
  • Managing performance for reporting and planning
  • Meeting retention and compliance requirements at lower cost

In the Autonomous Enterprise, the stakes are higher. Tiering decisions now influence:

  • Real-time operational analytics: e.g., S/4HANA operational KPIs, supply chain control towers, same‑day profitability
  • Tactical management reporting: month‑end/quarter‑end analysis, scenario planning, rolling forecasts
  • Strategic and historical analysis: multi‑year trends, structural cost analysis, sustainability reporting
  • AI and agentic use cases: Joule agents executing workflows, AI copilots assisting users in SAP Fiori and line-of-business apps,RAG‑style (retrieval‑augmented generation) scenarios pulling context into LLMs, ML models trained on long‑range historical, IoT, or behavioral data

Each of these has a different tolerance for latency, freshness, data volume, and cost. A well‑designed tiering strategy ensures that:

  • Hot data is available at in‑memory speed for agents and operational analytics
  • Warm data supports mid‑term analysis and planning without over‑provisioning HANA
  • Cold data and archives are kept cost‑efficiently but still discoverable, governable, and usable for ML and compliance

2. Data Tiering Concepts & Terminology:

Before going into SAP Datasphere specifics, it helps to establish a clear, neutral vocabulary for data tiering that you can reuse with architects, business stakeholders, and AI teams.

2.1 What Is Data Tiering?  Data tiering is a data management strategy where you separate data into different temperature tiers and storage technologies and core dimensions classifying it:

  1. Access frequency: How often is the data read? Hourly, daily, monthly, rarely?
  2. Update requirement: Does the data need to be updated after initial load? Is it transactional (insert/update/delete) or more append‑only?
  3. Performance & latency:  How fast do queries need to return? Does an AI agent or operational dashboard depend on sub‑second responses?
  4. Data Freshness:  Does the data need to be real‑time, near real‑time, or is daily/weekly refresh enough?
  5. Retention period: How many years must the data be kept for legal, audit, or business reasons?
  6. Data volume: How large is the dataset today, and how fast is it growing?
  7. Security & compliance: Are there specific regulations (GDPR, industry rules) that affect where data can be stored and who can access it?
  8. Business criticality (MOST IMPORTANT): If this data is slow or unavailable, what is the impact on the business and on AI‑driven processes?

In SAP terms, data tiering is part of multi‑temperature data management: you keep the most valuable, time‑critical data on your fastest (and most expensive) infrastructure, while moving less critical data to cheaper, slower layers without losing access to it.

2.2 The Classical 3 Tiers: Hot, Warm, Cold Most SAP data tiering discussions (HANA , BW/4HANA, Datasphere) use the same three core concepts, with slightly different technical realizations. Remote Data Tier is an data access pattern.

2.3 Data Tiering vs. Related Concepts in SAP: It is useful to separate data tiering from other, sometimes overlapping, concepts:

  • Data aging (e..g SAP S/4HANA): Framework that moves older data from hot in‑memory storage to warm disk‑based storage within the same HANA system. It reduces memory footprint while keeping data accessible. This is a mechanism that supports a tiering strategy at the application level.
  • Data Tiering Optimization (DTO) in BW/4HANA: Classifies data in an ADSO as hot, warm, or cold, and maps each temperature to a specific storage technology (e.g., HANA in‑memory, HANA NSE/extension nodes, SAP IQ). DTO is a tool that applies tiering policies in BW/4HANA.
  • Embedded Data Lake / data lake integration: Storing large volumes of structured/semi‑structured/unstructured data on low‑cost cloud object storage (e.g., embedded data lake in SAP Datasphere, or external data lakes). This typically supports warm/cold tiers for large volumes.
  • Virtualization / federation: Data remains in the source system (S/4HANA, Ariba, ECC, BW, non‑SAP DB, data lake) and is accessed on demand via remote tables or views. Virtualization is not a tier by itself, but an access pattern that can implement hot, warm, or cold tiers without physical replication.

2.4 Analytical & AI Consumption Patterns:  Tiering decisions should start from the intended consumption patterns, not from the storage technology. Key patterns include:

  • Real‑time operational analytics:  Examples: same‑day order‑to‑cash KPIs, supply chain control tower, production monitoring.Requirements: hot data, very low latency, typically in‑memory HANA, minimal joins across slow systems.
  • Tactical reporting & planning: Examples: monthly financial close, rolling forecasts, quarterly performance reviews. Requirements: mix of hot and warm data, acceptable latency in seconds; may combine data from replicated and virtual sources.
  • Strategic, historical, and regulatory analysis: Examples: multi‑year profitability, sustainability KPIs, root‑cause analysis over several years. Requirements: heavy use of warm and cold tiers; performance is important but not sub‑second; cost and retention dominate.
  • AI & ML training: Examples: training predictive models, building recommendation systems, anomaly detection, LLM fine‑tuning. Requirements: large volumes of warm/cold data; batch access; cost‑efficient storage more important than interactive speed.
  • AI & agent inference / context retrieval (RAG):  Examples: Joule or custom agents answering questions or taking actions using business data context. Requirements: hot data products with low latency and rich semantics; limited but well‑curated warm data for extended context; some cold data may be pre‑indexed or summarized.

Each pattern maps to a different  temperature mix which later translates into concrete options in SAP Datasphere (local tables (In-memory/Disk), remote tables, object storage (Delta-tables), etc.).

2.5 Temperature as a Policy, Not Just a Location: A subtle but important point: data temperature is a policy, not just a physical storage location.For example:

  1. A table partition might be “hot” for the current year and “warm” for previous years, even though the table itself still lives in SAP Datasphere.
  2. An object in an embedded data lake (Object store) might be “warm” when actively used for ML training, then become “cold” once the model is trained and only needs occasional retraining.
  3. Data can move hot → warm → cold as it ages (time‑based policies) or cold → warm → hot when certain events or AI scenarios increase its business relevance (usage‑based policies).

This policy‑driven view becomes especially important in SAP Datasphere and Business Data Cloud, where you design data products and spaces that must support changing business and AI requirements over time.

2.6 Data tiering characteristics: 

Data TierPerformanceCostData VolumeData UpdatesTypical Business Scenarios
HOTBest / Ultra-FastHighLow-MediumHighOperational reporting, AI Agents, Joule context, SAC dashboards, Open Orders, Current Inventory, Planning, Master Data
WARMMediumMediumMedium-HighHigh-MediumHistorical operational analytics (1–5 years), Supply Chain analytics, Procurement spend analysis, Customer analytics, Finance trending, AI/ML
COLDLowLowMassiveLow/NoneAudit, Compliance, Archived ERP/BW history, Long-term retention, Historical IoT data, Delta lake tables, AI/ML
REMOTEVariable (Source Dependent)MinimalLowVery HighVirtualized access to S/4HANA, BW/4HANA, SAP HANA, Snowflake, Databricks, Azure Fabric, data discovery and low-frequency access scenarios

 

3. SAP Datasphere Architecture and Strategy for Data Tiering: 

Data tiering in SAP Datasphere is built on top of SAP HANA Cloud and the Business Data Cloud architecture. At a high level, you combine local tables, spaces, storage options, and access patterns to implement hot, warm, and cold tiers without losing business context. Remote table will point to directly to source without copying for the remote tier data.

3.1 Key Building Blocks

  • Tenant Sizing/Configuration: Based on the requirements select the storage and memory.
  • Datasphere Spaces: Logical workspaces that isolate data, users, and data storage resources (memory, disk, file storage).You can dedicate spaces to domains (Finance, Supply Chain), regions, or workloads (AI lab vs. standard reporting).
  • Data Builder (Data engineering & Modeling): Remote/local tables, views, data flows, replication flows, transformation flow, Analytic model etc. This is where you design physical/virtual placement and thus tiering.
  • Connections & Data Federation Layer: Connects to SAP S/4HANA, BW/4HANA, ECC, SuccessFactors, Ariba, non‑SAP DBs, data lakes, and SaaS APIs. Foundation for virtual access (remote tables) and data replication the two main tiering levers.

3.2 Storage & Access Options Relevant for Tiering:  Under the hood, Datasphere uses SAP HANA Cloud as a database.

  • Local Tables: Physically replicate and store the data.
  • Remote Tables / Virtualization: : No replication; queries are pushed down to the source (e.g., S/4HANA, BW, external DB, data lake)
  • View Persistency: Persisted views/materializations in Datasphere to speed up complex logic. Often used as a performance buffer between raw inbound data and reporting, instead of keeping everything hot.

3.4. Data Tiering Options in SAP Datasphere

Remote Tier: Remote Tables that point to data in source systems and runs queries via federation without data copy.

Hot Tier: Local Tables in In-memory storage. Local tables physically stored in the SAP HANA Cloud database of your Datasphere tenant (column store).

Warm Tier: Local Tables in Disk storage. Local tables physically stored in the SAP HANA Native Storage Extension (NSE) is a built‑in warm data option in SAP HANA Cloud to keep data on disk instead of fully in memory. It combines disk‑based storage with the in‑memory to lower costs while keeping acceptable performance.

Cold Tier: Local Tables in File storage. Local tables(delta tables) physically stored in the object store (HANA Data Lake Files) of your Datasphere tenant for structured, semistructured, and unstructured data sets.

4. SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

5. Design Patterns & Typical Scenarios

5.1 Pattern 1 – Operational & Agentic Core with Remote, Hot, Warm, and Cold Tiers.

Intent: Support real-time operational decisions and AI agents with the latest business context, keep monthly and quarterly analytics on warm transactional history, and move multi-year data to cold lake storage for audit, compliance, and ML training.

Common scenarios:

  • S/4HANA‑centric processes: Finance, Order‑to‑Cash, Procure‑to‑Pay, inventory, production.
  • A relatively small set of promoted tables: Daily Revenue, Open Orders, Customer 360 Core, etc.
  • Monthly, quarterly, and yearly reporting on transactional history.
  • Operational dashboards, control towers, exception monitoring.
  • Joule/agent scenarios that need real‑time, deterministic context.
  • Audit, regulatory, and historical intelligence use cases.

Architecture view:

Remote tier (Remote tables):

  • Real-time operational data is accessed directly from source applications.This tier is used when the business needs the latest committed state without waiting for replication.
  • Best suited for: Critical real-operational reports, Control-tower dashboards, Time-sensitive AI agents

Warm + Hot, Data warehouse & database ( Local tables-Disk/In-memory )

  • Warm (default): most replicated tables from S/4 and other apps land on HANA disk/NSE. Older partitions of the same transactional tables (12–36 months), used for monthly/quarterly reporting and trend analysis.
  • Hot (explicit): only the most critical slices (e.g., current year postings, open documents, key master data, control tables) are switched to in‑memory for ultra‑fast queries.

Cold tier, Data Lake storage (Local tables-Files)

  • Acts as the foundation of the data fabric and Lake storage holds multi-year historical facts, logs, IoT data, archived detail, and deep history that is rarely accessed but required for audit or ML training.
  • Best suited for: Model/ML training and retraining, Rare deep-dive analytics and Audit and compliance

Design guidance

  • Keep schema, semantics, and business definitions consistent across remote, hot, warm, and cold so that data products can span all tiers seamlessly.
  • Use partitioning on the warm tier so that the active 12–36 month range behaves like “hot” for most analytical queries.
  • Separate your models by purpose:
    • Operational / real-time models should combine remote + hot + current warm.
    • Union models: Remote+Hot+Warm+Cold.
    • Historical / audit / ML models should extend into the cold tier only when needed.
  • Promote only the smallest business-critical slice to hot; keep the broader history warm and the full archive cold.

Example: 1. SAP Datasphere Native Connection with SAP S/4HANA: Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering 

Hot Tier: Current year data (2026)
Warm Tier: Previous two completed years (2024,2025)
Cold Tier: All data older (2023 and earlier)

1. UNIONing Sales Order Hot+Warm+Cold Tier Data, Fact Graphical View

2. Data Analysis View Showing - Analytic Model, Fact Model, Multi-Tier Tables and Replication Flow objects. 

3. Testing

Example: 2. SAP BDC Formation with SAP S/4HANA: Sales Order Data Product with below persisted data tiering 

5.2 Pattern 2 – Warm‑First Datasphere Core, Selectively Promoted → Hot/Cold

(Default warm in Datasphere, tuned over time up or down)

Intent: Start by loading everything needed for analytics into the warm tier of SAP Datasphere (default disk storage), then rebalance over time: move critical subsets up to hot and aging/low‑value data down to cold.

Common scenarios

  • Typical SAP Datasphere rollout where data replication lands in warm (Disk/HANA NSE) by default.
  • Traditional BI / reporting, planning, and first wave of AI use cases.
  • Customers that want simplicity first, then optimization.

Architecture view

Warm tier as default (Datasphere core Disk Storage)

  • All relevant operational and external data needed for reporting and AI is replicated/ingested into warm local tables.
  • Characteristics:
    • Good general‑purpose performance
    • Simple to model (everything is in one analytical environment)
    • Most dashboards, planning models, and initial agent use cases

Hot tier (promoted, latency‑critical slices)

  • After observing usage and SLAs, promote key tables and partitions to in‑memory hot for:
    • Mission‑critical dashboards and High‑frequency queries
    • AI agents needing sub‑second context
  • Typical candidates:
    • Current‑period transactional slices (e.g., last 30–90 days)
    • High‑value master data (customers, products, locations)
    • Control / security / data access lookup tables

Cold tier (demoted aging or low‑value data)

  • As data ages or is rarely accessed, move older partitions from warm into lake storage (HDFS/object store).
  • Characteristics:
    • Read‑only or minimal updates.
    • Year‑over‑year, long‑term trend analysis, audit and regulatory requirements
    • Occasional ML retraining on full history
  • The warehouse/lakehouse keeps a logical view that transparently spans warm + cold.

Remote tier (where appropriate): For some source systems, keep data remote and blend it with warm/hot only where necessary.

Design guidance

  • Start by designing semantic models and data products against the warm tier; this keeps onboarding simple.
  • Define lifecycle rules:
    • Age‑based: e.g., after 12-24 months in warm, move to cold.
    • Criticality‑based: e.g., if a dataset feeds a critical report/agent, promote its latest partitions to hot.
  • For AI:
    • Keep agent‑critical context (current state, key masters, control rules) in hot or the hottest partitions of warm.
    • Use warm for general analytics, trend analysis, and feature engineering.
    • Store long‑term histories in cold but link them semantically so agents and analysts can still reach them when necessary (e.g., via separate historical data products).

Example:  Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering: 

5.3 Pattern 3 – Cold‑First Datasphere Object Store, Selectively Promoted → Warm/Hot

(Everything lands in the lake, then is “pulled up” by importance)

Intent: Default to landing all data in the cold tier(cheap, scalable lake storage), then promote only critical data into warm and hot tiers based on actual access patterns and performance needs.

Common scenarios

  • High volume, low-moderate data updates, Mostly data batch processing (ingest → transform → aggregate).
  • Hourly, every 4-hrs, daily or even weekly once batch jobs.
  • Cloud‑scale data (IoT, clickstream, logs, partner feeds) combined with SAP business data.
  • Organizations with strong cost pressure and many unknown future use cases.
  • Heavy AI / ML experimentation and data science workloads.

Architecture view

Cold tier as landing zone (Local tables-Files)

  • All raw and detailed data (transactions, events, logs, IoT, external feeds) lands first in the HDLF/cold lake.
  • Acts as, long‑term system of record and single source for all higher tiers.
  • Delete Previous Versions: Local tables store high volume of data, including historical data. When data is updated, a new version is created and stored. Keeping all these versions consume a lot of memory, and can affect performance. You must clean them up once they have been consumed by flows to optimize storage costs and improve performance.

Warm tier (curated subsets in the lakehouse / warehouse)

  • Frequently used, cleansed, and modelled subsets are loaded from the lake into warm local tables in SAP Datasphere.
  • Characteristics:
    • Structured, governed, optimized for analytical workloads
    • Drives standard BI, tactical reporting, and many AI feature sets
  • Only a fraction of lake data is promoted here at any given time.

Hot tier (very selective, performance‑critical slices)

  • Small number of tables/partitions further promoted from warm to in‑memory hot for:
    • Executive dashboards
    • Time‑critical planning and simulations
    • High‑throughput AI inference and agents
  • Typically:
    • Recent periods (e.g., current month/quarter)
    • Narrow sets of attributes and key metrics

Remote tier (optional)

  • Some data may remain in source systems and be accessed virtually instead of being landed in the lake (e.g., SAP SaaS applications).
  • Treated logically as warm/cold depending on latency and SLA.

Design guidance

  • Treat the cold lake as default; movement to warm/hot must be justified by:
    • Access frequency
    • Latency requirements
    • Business criticality
  • Automate usage‑driven promotion:
    • Monitor query logs on lake and warm tables.
    • Identify top N datasets to copy or materialize in warm.
    • From warm, promote an even smaller top of top to hot.
  • For AI:
    • Training and experimentation pull broadly from the cold lake.
    • Production agents and data products should rely primarily on warm/hot, with controlled fallbacks to cold for rare, deep questions.

Example:  Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering:

6. Best practices and Recommendations for Data Tiering:  

Remote tier:

  • Use remote tables when you need real‑time S/4HANA, LoB Apps, or source data with no replication delay.
  • Keep remote queries narrow and push filters and aggregates down to the source. Always filter by time, company code, plant, etc. and Push filters and aggregates down to the source to avoid large data transfers.
  • Avoid overusing remote access for heavy self‑service; replicate to warm if queries become frequent or complex.
  • Use remote + local table together: Keep always‑hot context (small master data, control tables) in local hot and Join remote operational facts with local hot masters for fast, enriched views.
  • If a remote dataset becomes high‑volume or heavily used:  Replicate it into warm and optionally promote the most critical slice to hot.

Hot tier, In‑memory (ultra‑fast, mission‑critical):

When to use ?

  • Data powers mission‑critical dashboards, planning, or AI agents.
  • You need sub‑second to low‑second response and high concurrency.
  • Data changes frequently and is used continuously (daily / hourly).

What to put in hot ?

  • Current period slices of key transactional tables (e.g., last 30–90 days).
  • High‑value master data (Customer, Material, Plant, BP, cost center, etc.).
  • Control and data access control tables used in joins and authorization logic.
  • Persisted outputs of core semantic models and AI‑critical data products (e.g., Daily Revenue, Open Orders, Customer 360 Core).

Best practices:

  • Keep hot small and focused: Frequently accessed, Feeding critical reports or Joule/AI agents, needed for very recent periods.
  • Partition by time and business keys: Make only the most recent partitions hot; keep older ones warm/cold.
  • Materialize complex logic: Persist heavy joins and KPI logic into hot tables (via View persistency/Transformation Flows) instead of recomputing in every query.
  • Minimize width: Only store required columns; move verbose or rarely used attributes to warm/cold dimensions.
  • Protect hot capacity: Use separate spaces or roles to keep ad‑hoc/lab workloads off production hot tables.

Warm tier (HANA disk / NSE):

When to use? 

  • Default tier for replicated SAP and non‑SAP data in Datasphere.
  • You need good performance for 12–36 months of history, but not in‑memory speed.
  • Data is still updatable and frequently used for reporting and planning.

What to put in warm?

  • 1-2-3 years of transactional fact tables (FI, SD, MM, PP, CO, etc.) and less time‑critical master data and lookup tables.
  • Curated, harmonised reporting models that are widely reused.
  • Staging/curated tables used for feature engineering and AI training.

Best practices:

  • Treat warm as your default landing zone for replication.
  • SAP‑managed and customer‑managed data products can be replicated into local table Ideal for Monthly/quarterly reporting, Cross‑domain analytics or Standard AI feature sets and model training.
  • Use time‑based partitioning so:
    • Recent partitions (e.g., last 12–18 months) behave near‑hot.
    • Older partitions are candidates to move to cold.
  • Apply HANA Cloud warm‑store sizing rules and do not let warm grow uncontrolled.
  • Use warm as a “parking lot”:
    • Move data from hot → warm as it ages.
    • Keep still occasionally needed data here before sending it to cold.
  • Monitor usage:
    • Promote warm tables/partitions to hot if they become latency‑critical.
    • Demote rarely used warm data to cold to free capacity.

Cold tier (HDLF / object store):

When to use? 

  • Data is rarely accessed, mostly for audit, compliance, or deep historical analytics.
  • You need very low storage cost and can tolerate high latency.
  • Ideal for long‑term retention and cloud‑scale AI/ML training.

What to put in cold?

  • Detailed line‑item history older than 3–5+ years.
  • Full raw IoT, log, and event streams once aggregated metrics exist in warm/hot.
  • Long‑term snapshots, backups, and regulatory archives.
  • Large external datasets used mainly for ML experimentation.

Best practices:

  • Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store.
  • Design cold primarily for: Historical analytics, large‑slice ML training and forensic investigations.
